title: The immune system
paperName: Paper 1 Multiple Choice & Paper 2 AS Level Structured Questions
summary: Cambridge 9700 Paper 2 — The immune system (11.1). A-Level Notes diagram-backed lesson with premium structure and live visuals.
learningObjectives: Distinguish between specific and non-specific immunity, identifying key components of each system.
Explain the roles of B and T lymphocytes, including clonal selection and expansion, in specific immunity.
Describe antibody structure and function, differentiating between natural and artificial immunity, including the principles of vaccination.

simpleExplanation.title: Understanding Your Body's Defence
simpleExplanation.summary: Your immune system is your body's dedicated security force, constantly working to protect you from harmful invaders like bacteria and viruses. It uses a combination of general defences that act quickly and specific, targeted responses that remember past threats. Mastering this topic means understanding how your body identifies and neutralises these microscopic dangers.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of your immune system like a highly organised castle defence: Non-specific defences are the walls, moats, and guards (skin, mucus, phagocytes) that stop most invaders at the gate. Specific defences are the elite archers and strategists (lymphocytes and antibodies) who identify unique enemy banners, develop tailored attacks, and even remember them for future invasions.
steps: Start with the basics: non-specific vs. specific defences.
Dive into lymphocytes: B-cells, T-cells, and how they multiply.
Master antibody structure and their varied functions.
Review all types of immunity, paying special attention to vaccination.

sections.content: Welcome to this premium revision lesson on the **immune system** – your body's incredible defence network! It's a complex yet fascinating topic that underpins much of human health and disease, making it a frequent feature in your Cambridge 9700 Biology exams. We'll explore how your body distinguishes **self** from **non-self**, mounts both **non-specific** and **specific** responses to **pathogens**, and develops lasting **immunity**. Key points align with **[A-Level Notes](https://alevel-notes.weebly.com/biology.html)** 9700 Biology chapter **11** (topic **11.1**). Notes and diagrams from **[A-Level Notes](https://alevel-notes.weebly.com/biology.html)** chapter **11**, topic **11.1** (2 pages).

1. Non-Specific (Innate) Immunity: Your First Line of Defence

Your body's initial defence against pathogens is often called non-specific or innate immunity because it provides an immediate, generalised response, irrespective of the specific pathogen. These defences include:

*   **Mechanical Barriers**: Your **skin** acts as a tough, physical barrier, preventing most microorganisms from entering. **Mucous membranes** line respiratory, digestive, and urogenital tracts, trapping pathogens in sticky mucus. Cilia in the trachea and bronchi then sweep this mucus upwards to be swallowed or expelled.
*   **Chemical Barriers**: **Stomach acid** (hydrochloric acid) destroys most pathogens ingested with food. **Lysozyme**, an enzyme found in tears, saliva, and other secretions, breaks down bacterial cell walls. Some secretions also contain antimicrobial peptides.
*   **Cellular Defences**: **Phagocytes**, such as **macrophages** and **neutrophils**, are white blood cells that engulf and destroy pathogens through a process called **phagocytosis**. They are the 'clean-up crew' of the immune system. The **inflammatory response** is a localised tissue response to injury or infection, characterised by redness, heat, swelling, and pain. It brings phagocytes and other immune components to the site of infection.

sections.content: 2. Specific (Adaptive) Immunity: The Targeted Response

If non-specific defences are breached, specific immunity, also known as adaptive immunity, comes into play. This system is highly targeted, remembers past infections, and leads to long-term protection. It primarily involves **lymphocytes**, a type of white blood cell, which originate in the bone marrow and mature in different locations:

*   **B-lymphocytes (B-cells)**: Mature in the bone marrow. They are responsible for **humoral immunity**, producing **antibodies** that circulate in the blood and lymph to target extracellular pathogens and toxins.
*   **T-lymphocytes (T-cells)**: Mature in the thymus gland. They are responsible for **cell-mediated immunity**, directly attacking infected body cells or coordinating other immune cells.

When a specific **antigen** (a molecule, usually a protein or carbohydrate, on the surface of a pathogen) enters the body, it is recognised by a complementary B or T lymphocyte. This triggers:

1.  **Clonal Selection**: The antigen 'selects' and activates the specific lymphocyte clone with the complementary receptor.
2.  **Clonal Expansion**: The activated lymphocyte undergoes rapid mitotic division, producing many identical copies (clones) of itself. These clones differentiate into:
    *   **Effector cells**: B-cells differentiate into **plasma cells** that secrete vast quantities of antibodies. T-cells differentiate into **cytotoxic T-cells** (which kill infected cells), **helper T-cells** (which activate other immune cells), or suppressor T-cells.
    *   **Memory cells**: A subset of B and T cells that persist for a long time, providing a faster and stronger **secondary immune response** upon subsequent exposure to the same antigen.

items: Distinguish clearly between B-lymphocytes (humoral immunity, antibody production) and T-lymphocytes (cell-mediated immunity, direct cell killing or helper function).
Explain that specific immunity involves **clonal selection** (antigen binding to specific lymphocyte) followed by **clonal expansion** (rapid division).
Emphasise the role of **memory cells** in providing faster, stronger secondary responses upon re-exposure to the same antigen.
Understand that T-helper cells are crucial for activating both B-cells and cytotoxic T-cells, coordinating the overall specific immune response.

sections.content: 3. Antibodies: Structure and Function

Antibodies are Y-shaped proteins (immunoglobulins) produced by plasma cells in response to a specific antigen. Each antibody molecule has a distinct structure:

*   **Four Polypeptide Chains**: Two identical **heavy chains** and two identical **light chains**, held together by **disulfide bonds**.
*   **Variable Regions**: At the 'arms' of the Y, these regions have a unique amino acid sequence that forms the **antigen-binding site**, complementary to a specific antigen. This is where the antibody recognises and attaches to the pathogen.
*   **Constant Regions**: The rest of the antibody molecule, which determines the class of antibody and its effector functions.

Antibodies do not directly kill pathogens but rather mark them for destruction or neutralise them through several mechanisms:

*   **Agglutination**: Antibodies, having multiple binding sites, clump pathogens together, making them easier for phagocytes to engulf.
*   **Neutralisation**: Antibodies bind to bacterial toxins or the surface antigens of viruses, preventing them from attaching to host cells and causing harm.
*   **Opsonisation**: Antibodies coat the surface of pathogens, acting as 'labels' that make them more easily recognised and ingested by phagocytes.
*   **Complement Activation**: Antibodies can activate a cascade of plasma proteins (the complement system) that can directly lyse (burst) pathogens or enhance inflammation and phagocytosis.

sections.question: Outline how antibodies protect the body against pathogens, referring to at least two distinct mechanisms. [4 marks]
sections.solution:
1.  **Neutralisation**: Antibodies bind to toxins or viral surface proteins, preventing them from attaching to host cells and causing damage or infection. [1]
2.  **Agglutination**: Antibodies have multiple binding sites, allowing them to cross-link and clump together pathogens (e.g., bacteria), making them less mobile and easier targets for phagocytosis. [1]
3.  **Opsonisation**: Antibodies coat the surface of pathogens, acting as 'tags' that enhance their recognition and uptake by phagocytes (e.g., macrophages). [1]
4.  **Complement Activation**: Antibodies can bind to the pathogen surface and trigger the activation of the complement system, leading to the lysis of the pathogen or increased inflammation and phagocytosis. [1]
*(Award up to 4 marks for any two distinct mechanisms clearly explained, with specific examples or details of how the pathogen is affected.)*

sections.content: Examiners look for precise terminology. When discussing lymphocytes, clearly differentiate between B-lymphocytes (which differentiate into plasma cells to produce antibodies) and T-lymphocytes (which have cell-mediated roles like cytotoxic T-cells killing infected cells or T-helper cells activating other immune cells). Don't mix them up! Also, ensure you can define terms like 'clonal selection' and 'opsonisation' accurately and in context.
4. Types of Immunity and Vaccination

Immunity can be broadly categorised by how it is acquired and whether the body actively produces antibodies.

*   **Active Immunity**: The body's immune system produces its own antibodies and memory cells in response to an antigen. This provides long-term protection.
    *   **Natural Active Immunity**: Acquired after exposure to an actual pathogen (e.g., recovering from measles).
    *   **Artificial Active Immunity**: Acquired through **vaccination**, where attenuated (weakened), dead, or parts of pathogens (antigens) are introduced to stimulate an immune response without causing disease.
*   **Passive Immunity**: The body receives pre-formed antibodies from an external source. This provides immediate but short-term protection as no memory cells are formed.
    *   **Natural Passive Immunity**: Acquired when antibodies are passed from mother to child (e.g., across the placenta or via breast milk).
    *   **Artificial Passive Immunity**: Acquired through the injection of antibodies (e.g., antitoxins for tetanus or rabies antiserum).

**Vaccination programmes** are vital for public health. By introducing antigens, vaccines stimulate a primary immune response, leading to the formation of memory cells. This means that if the vaccinated individual encounters the actual pathogen, a rapid and strong secondary immune response can be mounted, preventing illness. Widespread vaccination also contributes to **herd immunity**, where a large proportion of the population is immune, making it difficult for the pathogen to spread and protecting those who cannot be vaccinated (e.g., infants, immunocompromised individuals).

items: Clearly define and provide distinct examples for all four types of immunity: natural active, natural passive, artificial active, and artificial passive.
Understand that **active immunity** involves the body producing its own antibodies and memory cells, providing long-term protection.
Know that **passive immunity** involves receiving pre-formed antibodies, offering immediate but short-term protection as no memory cells are formed.
Explain that **vaccination** induces artificial active immunity by introducing antigens to stimulate a primary immune response and form memory cells.
Recognise the importance of **herd immunity** in protecting vulnerable individuals within a population by reducing pathogen transmission.

sections.label: Mark a past-paper question on this topic

faq.q: What is the main difference between specific and non-specific immunity for Cambridge 9700 Biology?
faq.a: Non-specific immunity (innate) provides an immediate, general defence against *any* pathogen, using physical barriers, chemicals, and phagocytes. Specific immunity (adaptive) is a targeted, slower response that remembers *specific* pathogens for future encounters, involving lymphocytes and antibodies. Master these concepts with our premium course materials and practice past papers.

faq.q: How do B-lymphocytes and T-lymphocytes contribute to immunity in 9700 Biology?
faq.a: B-lymphocytes are responsible for **humoral immunity**, maturing into plasma cells that produce antibodies to neutralise pathogens in body fluids. T-lymphocytes are involved in **cell-mediated immunity**, with cytotoxic T-cells directly killing infected cells and helper T-cells coordinating other immune responses. Dive deeper into their distinct roles with our revision lessons!

faq.q: Why are vaccinations important for public health according to the Cambridge 9700 syllabus?
faq.a: Vaccinations induce artificial active immunity by exposing the body to antigens, leading to the formation of memory cells without causing disease. This provides long-term protection and contributes to **herd immunity**, where a high vaccination rate protects unvaccinated individuals by reducing pathogen transmission. Enhance your understanding with our comprehensive past paper analysis and free course content.
